author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm CXLVI

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 1. …Behold the Psalm soundeth; it is the voice of some one (and that some one are ye, if ye will), of some one encouraging his soul to praise God, and saying to himself, “Praise the Lord, O my soul” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For sometimes in the tribulations and temptations of this present life, whether we will or no, our soul is troubled; of which troubling he speaketh in another Psalm.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But to remove this troubling, he suggesteth joy; not as yet in reality, but in hope; and saith to it when troubled and anxious, sad and sorrowing, “Hope in God, for I will yet confess to Him.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But who saith it, and to whom saith he it?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What shall we say, brethren?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it the flesh that saith, “Praise thou the Lord, O my soul”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And can the flesh suggest good counsel to the soul?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: However much the flesh be conquered, and subjected as a servant to us through strength which the Lord imparteth, that it serve us entirely as a bond slave, enough for us that it hinder us not.…For the body, inasmuch as it is the body, is even beneath the soul; and every soul, however vile, is found more excellent than the most excellent body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And let not this seem to you to be wonderful, that even any vile and sinful soul is better than any great and most surpassing body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is better, not in deserts, but in nature.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The soul indeed is sinful, is stained with certain defilements of lusts; yet gold, though rusted, is better than the most polished lead.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let your mind then run over every part of creation, and ye will see that what we are saying is not incredible, that a soul, however blameable, is yet more praiseworthy than a praiseworthy body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There are two things, a soul and a body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The soul I chide, the body I praise: the soul I chide, because it is sinful; the body I praise, because it is sound.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Yet it is in its own kind that I praise the soul, and in its own kind that I blame the soul: and so in its own kind I praise the body, or blame it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If you ask me which is better, what I have blamed or what I have praised, wondrous is the answer thou wilt receive.…So you speak of the best horse and the worst man: yet thou preferrest the man thou findest fault with to the horse thou praisest.…The nature of the soul is more excellent than the nature of the body: it surpasseth it by far, it is a thing spiritual, incorporeal, akin to the substance of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is somewhat invisible, it ruleth the body, moveth the limbs, guideth the senses, prepareth thoughts, putteth forth actions, taketh in images of countless things; who is there, in short, beloved brethren, who may suffice for the praises of the soul?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And yet such is the grace given to it, that this man saith, “Praise the Lord, O my soul.”…It is not the flesh that saith it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let the body be angel-like, still it is inferior to the soul, it cannot give advice to its superior.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The flesh when duly obedient is the handmaid of the soul: the soul rules, the body obeys; the soul commands, the body performs; how then can the flesh give this advice to the soul?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it then perchance the soul herself, who saith to herself, and in a manner commandeth herself, and exhorteth and asketh herself?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For through certain passions in one part of her nature she wavered; but in another part, which they call the reasonable mind, the wisdom whereby she thinks, clinging to God, and now sighing towards Him, she perceives that certain inferior parts of her are troubled by worldly emotions, and by a certain excitement of earthly desires, betake them to outward things, leaving God who is within; so she recalleth herself from things outward to inward, from lower to higher, and says, “Praise the Lord, O my soul.”…The soul itself giveth itself counsel from the light of God by the reasonable mind, whereby it conceiveth the wisdom fixed in the everlasting nature of its Author.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It readeth there of somewhat to be feared, to be praised, to be loved, to be longed for, and sought after: as yet it graspeth it not, it comprehendeth it not; it is, as it were, dazzled with brightness; it has not strength to abide there.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore it gathers itself, as it were, into a sound state, and saith, “Praise the Lord, O my soul.”…And then the soul, weighed down, as it were, and unable to stand up as is fitting, answereth the mind, “I will praise the Lord in my life” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is, “in my life”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because now I am in my death.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore first encourage thyself, and say, “Praise the Lord, O my soul.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thy soul answereth thee, I do praise so far as I can, slightly, poorly, weakly.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Because, “while we are in the body, we are absent from the Lord.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “In my life.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now what has it?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It might answer thee, “My death.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence, “My death”? because I am absent from the Lord.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if to cling to Him is life, to depart from Him is death.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what comforteth thee?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now thou livest in hope: in hope praise, in hope sing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thy death is from the sadness of this life, thou livest in hope of a future life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And how wilt thou praise thy Lord?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I will sing unto my God, as long as I have my being.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What sort of praise is this, “I will sing unto my God as long as I have being”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold, my brethren, what sort of being this will be; where there will be everlasting praise, there will be also everlasting being.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold, now thou hast being: dost thou sing unto God as long as thou hast being?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold, thou wast singing, and hast turned thyself away to some business, thou singest no longer, yet thou hast being: thou hast being, yet thou singest not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It may be also thy desire turneth thee to somewhat; not only dost thou not sing, but thou even offendest His ears, yet thou hast being.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What praise will that be, when thou praisest as long as thou hast being?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what meaneth, “as long as I have being”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Will there be any time when he will not be?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nay, rather, that “long” will be everlasting, and therefore it will be truly “long.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For whatever hath end in time, however prolonged it is, is yet not “long.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Put not your trust in princes” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Brethren, here we receive a mighty task; it is a voice from heaven, from above it soundeth to us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For now through some kind of weakness the soul of man, whensoever it is in tribulation here, despaireth of God, and chooseth to rely on man.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let it be said to one when set in some affliction, “There is a great man, by whom thou mayest be set free;” he smileth, he rejoiceth, he is lifted up.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if it is said to him, “God freeth thee,” he is chilled, so to speak, by despair.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The aid of a mortal is promised, and thou rejoicest; the aid of the Immortal is promised, and art thou sad?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is promised thee that thou shalt be freed by one who needeth to be freed with thee, and thou exultest, as at some great aid: thou art promised that Liberator, who needeth none to free Him, and thou despairest, as though it were but a fable.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Woe to such thoughts: they wander far; truly there is sad and great death in them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Approach, begin to long, begin to seek and to know Him by whom thou wast made.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For He will not leave His work, if He be not left by His work.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 5. …“His breath shall go forth, and he shall return to his earth: in that day shall all his thoughts perish” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Where is swelling? where is pride? where is boasting?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But perhaps he will have passed to a good place, if indeed he have passed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For I know not whither he who spake thus hath passed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he spake in pride; and I know not whither such men pass, save that I look into another Psalm, and see that their passage is an evil one.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I beheld the wicked lifted up above the cedars of Libanus, and I passed by, and, lo, he was not; and I sought him, and his place was not found.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The good man, who passed by, and found not the wicked, reached a place where the wicked is not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore, brethren, let us all listen: brethren, beloved of God, let us all listen; in whatsoever tribulation, in whatsoever longing for the heavenly gift, “let us not trust in princes, nor in sons of men, in whom is no salvation.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All this is mortal, fleeting, perishable.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What then must we do, if we are not to hope in sons of men, nor in princes?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What must we do?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Blessed is he whose Helper is the God of Jacob” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 5): not this man or that man; not this angel or that angel; but, “blessed is he whose Helper is the God of Jacob:” for to Jacob also so great an Helper was He, that of Jacob He made him Israel.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: O mighty help! now he is Israel, “seeing God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: While then thou art placed here, and a wanderer not yet seeing God, if thou hast the God of Jacob for thy Helper, from Jacob thou wilt become Israel, and wilt be “seeing God,” and all toil and all groans shall come to an end, gnawing cares shall cease, happy praises shall succeed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Blessed is he whose Helper is the God of Jacob;” of this Jacob.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore is he happy?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Meanwhile, while yet groaning in this life, “his hope is in the Lord his God.”…Who is this, “Lord his God”?…“To us there is one God, the Father, of whom are all things, and one Lord Jesus Christ, through whom are all things.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore let Him be thy hope, even the Lord thy God; in Him let thy hope be.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: His hope too is in the lord his god, who worshippeth Saturn; his hope is in the lord his god, who worshippeth Neptune or Mercury; yea more, I add, who worshippeth his belly, of whom is said, “whose god is their belly.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The one is the god of the one, the other of the other.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Who is this “blessed” one? for “his hope is in the Lord his God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But who is He?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Who made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that is in them” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: My brethren, we have a great God; let us bless His holy Name, that He hath deigned to make us His possession.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As yet thou seest not God; thou canst not fully love what as yet thou seest not.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All that thou seest, He hath made.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou admirest the world; why not the Maker of the world?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou lookest up to the heavens, and art amazed: thou considerest the whole earth, and tremblest; when canst thou contain in thy thought the vastness of the sea?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Look at the countless number of the stars, look at all the many kind of seeds, all the different sorts of animals, all that swimmeth in the water, creepeth on the earth, flieth in the sky, hovereth in the air; how great are all these, how beautiful, how fair, how amazing!

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold, He who made all these, is thy God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Put thy hope in Him, that thou mayest be happy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “His hope is in the Lord his God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Observe, my brethren, the mighty God, the good God, who maketh all these things.…If he mentioned these things only, perhaps thou wouldest answer me, “God, who made heaven and earth and sea, is a great God: but doth He think of me?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It would be said to thee, “He made thee.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How so? am I heaven, or am I earth, or am I sea?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Surely it is plain; I am neither heaven, nor earth, nor sea: yet I am on earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: At least thou grantest me this, that thou art on earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hear then, that God made not only heaven and earth and sea: for He “made heaven and earth and sea, and all that is in them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If then He made all that is in them, He made thee also.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is too little to say, thee; the sparrow, the locust, the worm, none of these did He not make, and He careth for all.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: His care refers not to His commandment, for this commandment He gave to man alone.…As regards then the tenor of the commandment, “God doth not take care for oxen:” as regards His providential care of the universe, whereby He created all things, and ruleth the world, “Thou, Lord, shalt save both man and beast.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Here perhaps some one may say to me, “God careth not for oxen,” comes from the New Testament: “Thou, Lord, shalt save both man and beast,” is from the Old Testament.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There are some who find fault and say, that these two Testaments agree not with one another.…Let us hear the Lord Himself, the Chief and Master of the Apostles: “Consider,” saith He, “the fowls of the air; they sow not, neither do they reap, nor gather into barns, and your heavenly Father feedeth them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore even beside men, these animals are objects of care to God, to be fed, not to receive a law.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As far then as regards giving a law, “God careth not for oxen:” as regards creating, feeding, governing, ruling, all things have to do with God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Are not two sparrows sold for one farthing?” saith our Lord Jesus Christ, “and one of them shall not fall to the ground without the will of your Father: how much better are ye than they.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Perhaps thou sayest, God counteth me not in this great multitude.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There follows here a wondrous passage in the Gospel: “the hairs of your head are all numbered.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Who keepeth truth for ever.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What “truth for ever”? what “truth” doth He “keep,” and wherein doth “He keep it for ever”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Who executeth judgment for them that suffer wrong” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He avengeth them that suffer wrong.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There cometh at once to thee the voice of the Apostle: “now therefore there is altogether a fault among you, that ye go to law one with another: why do ye not rather suffer wrong?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He urged thee not to suffer annoyance, but to suffer wrong: for not every annoyance is wrong.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For whatever thou sufferest lawfully is not a wrong; lest perchance thou shouldest say, I also am among those who have suffered wrong, for I have suffered such a thing in such a place, and such a thing for such a reason.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Consider whether thou hast suffered a wrong.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Robbers suffer many things, but they suffer no wrong.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wicked men, evil doers, house-breakers, adulterers, seducers, all these suffer many evils, yet is there no wrong.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is one thing to suffer wrong; it is another to suffer tribulation, or penalty, or annoyance, or punishment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Consider where thou art; see what thou hast done; see why thou art suffering; and then thou seest what thou art suffering.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Right and wrong are contraries.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Right is what is just.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For not all that is called right, is right.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What if a man lay down for you unjust right? nor indeed is it to be called right, if it is unjust.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That is true right, which is also just.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Consider what thou hast done, not what thou art suffering.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If thou hast done right, thou art suffering wrong; if thou hast done wrong, thou art suffering right.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Who giveth food to the hungry.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold, from thee I look for nothing: “God giveth food to the hungry.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Who are “the hungry”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is, all?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To all things that have life, to all men He giveth food: doth He not reserve some food for His beloved?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If they have another kind of hunger, they have also another kind of food.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let us first enquire what their hunger is, and then we shall find their food.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Blessed are they that hunger and thirst after righteousness, for they shall be filled.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We ought to be God’s hungry ones.…“The Lord looseth them that are fettered; the Lord lifteth up them that are dashed down; the Lord maketh wise them that are blind” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Perfectly hath he by this last sentence explained to us all the preceding ones: lest perchance, when he had said, “the Lord looseth them that are fettered,” we should refer it to those fettered ones, who for some crime are bound in irons by their masters: and in that he said, “He lifteth up them that are dashed down,” there should occur to our minds some one stumbling or falling, or thrown from a horse.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is another kind of fall, there are other kinds of fetters, just as there is other darkness and other light.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whereas he said, “He maketh the blind wise;” he would not say, He enlightened the blind, lest thou shouldest understand this also in reference to the flesh, as the man was enlightened by the Lord, when He anointed his eyes with clay made with spittle, and so healed him: that thou mightest not look for anything of this sort, when He is speaking of spiritual things, he pointeth to a sort of light of wisdom, wherewith the blind are enlightened.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore in the same way as the blind are enlightened with the light of wisdom, so are the fettered set free, and those who are dashed down are lifted up.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whereby then have we been fettered? whereby dashed down?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Our body was once an ornament to us: now, we have sinned, and thereby have had fetters put on us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What are our fetters?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Our mortality.…“The Lord loveth the righteous.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And who are the “righteous”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How far are they righteous now?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Just as thou hast; “the Lord, guardeth proselytes” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Proselytes” are strangers.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Every Church of the Gentiles is a stranger.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it cometh in to the Fathers, not sprung of their flesh, but their daughter by imitating them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Yet the Lord, not any man, guardeth them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The orphan and widow He will take up.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let none think that He taketh up the orphan for his inheritance, or the widow for any business of hers.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: True, God doth help them; and in all the duties of the human race, he doeth a good work, who taketh care of an orphan, who abandoneth not a widow: but in a certain way we are all orphans, not because our Father is dead, but because He is absent.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And the way of sinners He shall root out.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is, “the way of sinners”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To mock at these things which we say.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Who is an orphan, who a widow?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What kingdom of heaven, what punishment of hell is there?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These are fables of the Christians.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To what I see, to that will I live: “let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Beware lest such men persuade you of aught: let them not enter through your ears into your heart; let them find thorns in your ears: let him, who seeketh to enter thus, go away pierced: for “evil communications corrupt good manners.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But here perhaps thou wilt say, “Wherefore then are they prosperous?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold, they worship not God, and commit every kind of evil daily: yet they abound in those things, through want of which I toil.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Be not envious against sinners.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What they receive, thou seest; what is in store for them, seest thou not?…Wilt thou not believe even the Lord thy God, who saith, “Broad and spacious is the way that leadeth to destruction, and many there be that walk by it”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This “way the Lord will root out.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And, when “the way of sinners” has been “rooted out,” what remaineth for us?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Come, ye blessed of My father, enjoy the Kingdom;” “The Lord shall reign for ever” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “O Sion, thy God” shall reign for ever; surely thy God will not reign without thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For generation and generation.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He hath said it twice, because he could not say it for ever.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And think not that eternity is bounded by finite words.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The word eternity consists of four syllables; in itself it is without end.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It could not be commended to thee, save thus, “for generation and generation.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Too little hath he said: if he spoke it all day long, it were too narrow: if he spoke it all his life, must he not at length hold his peace?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Love eternity: without end shalt thou reign, if Christ be thine End, with whom thou shalt reign for ever and ever.
